questions.question: Which of the following is the primary objective of the Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005?
questions.option_1: To control corruption in the judiciary
questions.option_2: To promote transparency and accountability in government functioning
questions.option_3: To ensure reservations for backwards classes
questions.option_4: To regulate foreign investment in India
questions.solution:
Primary Objective of RTI Act

The Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005 was enacted to empower citizens, promote transparency, and ensure accountability in the working of every public authority. It seeks to contain corruption and make our democracy work for the people in a real sense by providing a legal right to request information from the government.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a mode of acquiring Indian citizenship under the Citizenship Act, 1955?
questions.solution:
Modes of Acquiring Citizenship

Under the Citizenship Act, 1955, there are five ways to acquire Indian citizenship: Birth, Descent, Registration, Naturalisation, and Incorporation of Territory. While 'adoption' is recognized under personal laws for other purposes, it is not a distinct category for acquiring citizenship under the 1955 Act.

questions.question: Who among the following is the appointing authority of District Judges in India?
questions.solution:
Appointment of District Judges

Under Article 233, the Governor of the State makes appointments, postings, and promotions of District Judges. This is done in consultation with the High Court exercising jurisdiction in relation to such State.

questions.question: Who is responsible for implementing the RTI Act in public departments?
questions.solution:
Central Public Information Officer (CPIO)

Every public authority is mandated to designate a Central Public Information Officer (CPIO) or State Public Information Officer (SPIO). These officers are responsible for processing RTI requests and providing information to the applicants within the statutory timeframe.

questions.question: In the case of Vihaan Kumar vs. State of Haryana, what constitutional violation led the Supreme Court to declare the arrest illegal?
questions.option_2: Violation of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
questions.option_3: Violation of Article 22(1) (Failure to inform grounds of arrest)
questions.solution:
Article 22(1): Rights of Arrested Persons

In cases like Vihaan Kumar vs. State of Haryana, the judiciary emphasizes Article 22(1), which mandates that no person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed of the grounds for such arrest as soon as may be. Failure to do so renders the arrest and subsequent detention illegal.

questions.question: To be eligible for membership of a State Legislature, a candidate must meet which of the following qualifications as laid down in the Representation of the People Act, 1951?
questions.option_1: Pay income tax in the State
questions.option_2: Be an elector (voter) in any State assembly constituency
questions.option_3: Be a permanent resident of the State for 15 years
questions.option_4: Have at least a graduate degree
questions.solution:
Eligibility for State Legislature

According to the Representation of the People Act, 1951, a person seeking to be a member of a State Legislative Assembly must be an elector (voter) for any Assembly constituency in that specific State. Residency for a fixed number of years or tax-paying status are not constitutional requirements for candidacy.

questions.question: Which Article deals with the constitution of Municipalities?
questions.solution:
Article 243Q: Constitution of Municipalities

Article 243Q provides for the constitution of three types of Municipalities: Nagar Panchayats (for transitional areas), Municipal Councils (for smaller urban areas), and Municipal Corporations (for larger urban areas).

questions.question: Which Constitutional Amendment Act added the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution of India?
questions.solution:
Ninth Schedule and First Amendment

The First Constitutional Amendment Act (1951) added the Ninth Schedule to protect land reform and other laws from judicial review on the ground that they violated Fundamental Rights. It was introduced to bypass legal hurdles in the implementation of the Zamindari abolition.

questions.question: Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to make laws relating to the acquisition and termination of the Indian Citizenship?
questions.solution:
Article 11: Parliament’s Power over Citizenship

While Articles 5 to 10 deal with citizenship at the time of the Constitution's commencement, Article 11 gives Parliament the power to make any provision with respect to the acquisition and termination of citizenship and all other matters relating to citizenship.

questions.question: Which Indian judgment laid down the concept of ‘Basic Structure’ of the Constitution?
questions.option_2: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
questions.option_3: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
questions.option_4: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
questions.solution:
Basic Structure Doctrine

The concept of the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution was established by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) case. It prevents Parliament from using its amending power to destroy the essential features of the Constitution.

questions.question: Who among the following is eligible to be appointed as the Chairperson of the Lokpal, as per the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013?
questions.option_2: A sitting judge of a High Court
questions.option_3: A retired Cabinet Secretary of India
questions.option_4: A former Judge of the Supreme Court
questions.solution:
Chairperson of the Lokpal

To ensure high integrity and judicial experience, the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 stipulates that the Chairperson should be a person who is or has been a Chief Justice of India, or a Judge of the Supreme Court, or an eminent person with impeccable integrity and expertise.

questions.question: Which Schedule of the Constitution deals with the administration of Scheduled Areas in states, other than the Northeast?
questions.solution:
Fifth Schedule: Scheduled Areas

The Fifth Schedule of the Constitution provides for the administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in states other than Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram (which are covered under the Sixth Schedule).

questions.question: The 91ˢᵗ Amendment of the Indian Constitution was enacted in which year?
questions.solution:
The 91st Amendment Act, 2003

Enacted in 2003, the 91st Amendment limited the size of the Council of Ministers to 15% of the House strength and strengthened the Anti-Defection Law by removing the 'split' provision (1/3rd members) as a defense against disqualification.

questions.question: What is the tenure duration of Justice BR Gavai as the Chief Justice of India?
questions.solution:
Justice B.R. Gavai’s Tenure

The tenure of a Chief Justice of India depends on their date of retirement (65 years). Following the seniority convention, Justice B.R. Gavai is slated to serve as the Chief Justice of India for a relatively short duration, specifically 6 months (from May 2025 to November 2025).

questions.question: In which of the following years was the Central Goods and Services Tax Act enacted?
questions.solution:
CGST Act, 2017

The Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act was enacted in 2017. This Act, along with the IGST and SGST Acts, replaced a multitude of indirect taxes to implement the 'One Nation, One Tax' regime starting from July 1, 2017.

questions.question: Which of these is a distinct feature of a statutory corporation in India?
questions.option_1: Its employees are considered government civil servants under the usual rules.
questions.option_2: It is fully owned by private shareholders and receives no government funding.
questions.option_3: It has no legal existence separate from the government.
questions.option_4: It is established by an Act, has a separate legal identity, and can sue or be sued.
questions.solution:
Statutory Corporations

A Statutory Corporation (like LIC or SBI) is a body corporate established by a special Act of Parliament. It is a separate legal entity from the government, can sue and be sued, and has its own independent financial structure and personnel policy.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.option_1: It deals with criminal appeals only
questions.option_2: It settles disputes between the Union and states
questions.option_4: It reviews the decisions of tribunals
questions.solution:
Original Jurisdiction: Federal Disputes

The Original Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court (Article 131) is primarily concerned with federal disputes. This includes legal conflicts between the Union and one or more States, or between two or more States, which cannot be heard by any other court first.

questions.question: In a State government, a minister holds office:
questions.option_1: during the pleasure of the President
questions.option_2: during the pleasure of the Governor
questions.option_4: as long as they enjoy the confidence of the State Legislative Council
questions.solution:
Pleasure of the Governor

Under Article 164, the Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor, and other ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister. Ministers hold office during the pleasure of the Governor, though in practice, this depends on their majority in the assembly.

questions.question: What does the Constitution of India stipulate regarding the composition of the Election Commission of India (ECI)?
questions.option_1: It must include one Election Commissioner from each major state to ensure federal balance
questions.option_2: It must always be a three-member body (one Chief Election Commissioner and two Election Commissioners).
questions.option_3: It may be single or multi-member; initially, there was only a Chief Election Commissioner until 1989.
questions.option_4: It is constitutionally fixed as a single-member body headed by the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) only.
questions.solution:
Composition of the ECI

The Election Commission may be a single-member or multi-member body as determined by the President. It was a single-member body until 1989, briefly became multi-member, reverted to single-member in 1990, and has been a three-member body (1 CEC and 2 ECs) consistently since October 1993.

questions.question: Which of the following courts is known as the 'Guardian of the Constitution' in India?
questions.solution:
Guardian of the Constitution

The Supreme Court of India is regarded as the 'Guardian of the Constitution' because it has the ultimate power of interpreting the document and the power of Judicial Review to ensure that no law or executive action violates constitutional mandates.

questions.question: Which body is responsible for recruitment to All India Services like IAS and IPS?
questions.solution:
Union Public Service Commission (UPSC)

The UPSC is the central recruiting agency in India. Under Article 315, it is responsible for conducting examinations and recruiting candidates for All India Services (IAS, IPS, IFS) and Central Services (Group A and Group B).

questions.question: The First Backward Classes Commission was appointed in which year?
questions.solution:
First Backward Classes Commission

The First Backward Classes Commission was appointed in 1953 under the chairmanship of Kaka Kalelkar. It was tasked with identifying socially and educationally backward classes and recommending measures for their advancement.

questions.question: The 105ᵗʰ Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021, is related to which of the following?
questions.option_1: Powers of state governments to identify OBCs
questions.option_3: Creation of a National Judicial Commission
questions.solution:
105th Amendment Act, 2021

The 105th Amendment restored the power of State Governments and Union Territories to identify and notify their own lists of Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs), which had been put into question by a previous Supreme Court interpretation.

questions.question: Which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India empowers the President to declare Scheduled Areas?
questions.solution:
Article 244: Scheduled Areas

Article 244 in Part X of the Constitution deals with the administration of Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas. It empowers the President to declare an area as a 'Scheduled Area' by an order, which ensures special administrative protections for the residents.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) of the Indian Parliament?
questions.option_2: To assist in the election of the Speaker
questions.solution:
Public Accounts Committee (PAC)

The PAC is the 'watchdog' of the Parliament's finances. Its primary role is to review the audit reports of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India to ensure that the money granted by Parliament has been spent by the government within the scope of the demand.
